Study C · Simulation animation

Phase separation and liquid–vapour coexistence

The full run over which the departure assessment is built: an initially uniform, metastable fluid at reduced temperature \(T_r=0.9\) separates into liquid and vapour domains, and the coexistence densities relax onto the Maxwell (equal-area) values.

What the animation demonstrates
  • Correct coexistence. The maximum and minimum reduced density settle onto the Maxwell-construction values for \(T_r=0.9\), confirming the equation of state and interface model reproduce the expected liquid–vapour densities.
  • Mass conservation. The global mass error decays and stays bounded, so the phase separation is not an artefact of a leaking scheme.
  • Interface capture. The density field develops sharp, stable liquid–vapour interfaces and the domains coarsen — the surface-tension model holds the interface together without it smearing or breaking up.
Coexistence targets (reduced units)
Reduced temperature
\(T_r = 0.9\)
Maxwell vapour density
\(\rho_v/\rho_{cr} = 0.4257\)
Maxwell liquid density
\(\rho_l/\rho_{cr} = 1.6573\)
Density ratio
\(\rho_l/\rho_v \approx 3.9\)
Run length
\(t = 0 \to 24{,}000\) time steps
Why this matters for Study C
Before the solver is judged on a bubble-departure diameter, it has to reproduce the right liquid–vapour coexistence and hold a stable interface. This run is that check: correct Maxwell densities, bounded mass error, and clean interface capture are the preconditions for trusting the departure result.
